text: LAB 1 EXPERIMENT 1: RC COUPLED COMMON EMITTER AMPLIFIER USING BC547 AIM - To design and study a single stage RC coupled common emitter amplifier using a BC547 transistor and to determine its voltage gain, input impedance = 2 kΩ, frequency response, and bandwidth using a sine wave input signal. APPARATUS REQUIRED - 1. Regulated DC power supply 2. Functiongenerator 3. CRO / DSO 4. multimeter 5. Breadboard 6. Connectingwires 7. BC547 transistor 8. Resistors and capacitors THEORY -  An RC coupled amplifier is a voltage amplifier in which resistors and capacitors are used to couple the AC signal from input to output while blocking DC components. The coupling capacitors prevent DC from one stage affecting the biasing of another stage.  In a common emitter amplifier, the emitter terminal is common to both input and output. The input signal is applied at the base and the output is taken from the collector. This configuration provides high voltage gain

another stage.  In a common emitter amplifier, the emitter terminal is common to both input and output. The input signal is applied at the base and the output is taken from the collector. This configuration provides high voltage gain and introduces a phase inversion of 180 degrees between input and output.  The transistor is biased using a voltage divider bias network. This keeps the transistor operating in the active region so that a small change in base voltage causes a large change in collector current. The collector resistor converts these current variations into voltage variations, producing amplification.  When a sine wave signal is applied at the input, it causes variations in base current. These variations produce larger variations in collector current, resulting in an amplified output signal at the collector. pg.1-I 24BEC064  The gain of an RC coupled amplifier depends on frequency.At low frequencies, the reactance of coupling and bypass capacitors is high, reducing gain. At

variations in collector current, resulting in an amplified output signal at the collector. pg.1-I 24BEC064  The gain of an RC coupled amplifier depends on frequency.At low frequencies, the reactance of coupling and bypass capacitors is high, reducing gain. At mid frequencies, the gain remains nearly constant. At high frequencies, gain decreases due to internal transistor capacitances and the Miller effect. PROCEDURE – 1. Assemble the RC coupled common emitter amplifier circuit on a breadboard as per the circuit diagram. 2. Apply a DC supply voltage of 15 V to the circuit. 3. Connect the function generator to the input through the coupling capacitor. 4. Apply a sine wave input of 500 mV peak-to-peak. 5. Observe the input and output waveforms on the CRO. 6. Measure the output peak-to-peak voltage. 7. Vary the input frequency from low to high. 8. Record the output voltage at each frequency. 9. Switch off the power supply after completing the experiment. pg.1-II 24BEC064 COMPONENTS

waveforms on the CRO. 6. Measure the output peak-to-peak voltage. 7. Vary the input frequency from low to high. 8. Record the output voltage at each frequency. 9. Switch off the power supply after completing the experiment. pg.1-II 24BEC064 COMPONENTS USED – Component Value Transistor BC547 Collector resistor 1.5 kΩ Emitter resistor 1 kΩ Base resistor R1 100 kΩ Base resistor R2 18 kΩ Input capacitor 1 µF Output capacitor 1 µF Emitter bypass capacitor 22 µF Supply voltage 12 V Parameter Value Signaltype Sine wave Input voltage 500 mV p-p Frequency range 1 Hz – 10 MHz OBSERVATION TABLE - Frequency Gain (Approx) Output Voltage (V p-p) 50 Hz 24.4 8.35 100 Hz 27.1 11.4 150 Hz 28.2 13.6 200 Hz 28.2 13.9 500 Hz 28.3 14.1 1 kHz 28.3 14.1 2 kHz 28.4 14.2 5 kHz 28.4 14.2 10 kHz 28.4 14.2 50 kHz 28.4 14.2 100 kHz 28.4 14.2 200 kHz 28.4 14.2 400 kHz 27.9 13.9

F =  Bandwidth kHz ≈ DISCUSSION – The RC coupled common emitter amplifier shows variation of gain with frequency.At low frequencies, gain is low due to the high reactance of coupling and bypass capacitors.As frequency increases, the gain increases and becomes almost constant in the mid-frequency range. In the midband region, the amplifier provides maximum gain, indicating proper biasing and stable operation in the active region. At high frequencies, gain decreases due to internal transistor capacitances and the Miller effect. The large output voltage near midband also causes slight reduction in gain as the output approaches supply limits. LAB 1
EXPERIMENT 1: RC COUPLED COMMON EMITTER AMPLIFIER USING
BC547
AIM - To design and study a single stage RC coupled common emitter amplifier
using a BC547 transistor and to determine its voltage gain, input impedance = 2 kΩ,
frequency response, and bandwidth using a sine wave input signal.

Functiongenerator

4. multimeter

THEORY -
 An RC coupled amplifier is a voltage amplifier in which resistors and capacitors
are used to couple the AC signal from input to output while blocking DC
components.

The coupling capacitors prevent DC from one stage affecting the
biasing of another stage.

In a common emitter amplifier, the emitter terminal is common to both input and
output.

The input signal is applied at the base and the output is taken from the
collector.

This configuration provides high voltage gain and introduces a phase
inversion of 180 degrees between input and output.
The transistor is biased using a voltage divider bias network.

This keeps the
transistor operating in the active region so that a small change in base voltage
causes a large change in collector current.

The collector resistor converts these
current variations into voltage variations, producing amplification.

When a sine wave signal is applied at the input, it causes variations in base
current.

These variations produce larger variations in collector current, resulting
in an amplified output signal at the collector.
The gain of an RC coupled amplifier depends on frequency.

At low frequencies,
the reactance of coupling and bypass capacitors is high, reducing gain.

At mid
frequencies, the gain remains nearly constant.

At high frequencies, gain
decreases due to internal transistor capacitances and the Miller effect.

Assemble the RC coupled common emitter amplifier circuit on a breadboard
as per the circuit diagram.
2. Apply a DC supply voltage of 15 V to the circuit.
3. Connect the function generator to the input through the coupling capacitor.
4. Apply a sine wave input of 500 mV peak-to-peak.
5. Observe the input and output waveforms on the CRO.
6. Measure the output peak-to-peak voltage.

7. Vary the input frequency from low to high.
8.
Record the output voltage at each frequency.
9. Switch off the power supply after completing the experiment.

DISCUSSION –
The RC coupled common emitter amplifier shows variation of gain with
frequency.

At low frequencies, gain is low due to the high reactance of coupling and
bypass capacitors.

As frequency increases, the gain increases and becomes almost
constant in the mid-frequency range.

In the midband region, the amplifier provides maximum gain, indicating proper
biasing and stable operation in the active region.

The large
output voltage near midband also causes slight reduction in gain as the output
approaches supply limits.

CONCLUSION –
 RC coupled common emitter amplifier was successfully designed and tested
 High voltage gain with phase inversion was obtained


Gain remains constant in mid-frequency range


Gain decreases at low and high frequencies


Experimental results match expected behavior
PRECAUTIONS –
 Check BC547 pin configuration before connecting
 Do not exceed rated supply voltage


small
 Observe correct polarity of electrolytic capacitors

Experiment 1: RC Coupled Common Emitter Amplifier. EXPERIMENT 1: RC COUPLED COMMON EMITTER AMPLIFIER. The primary subject of the document, an experiment to design and study a single stage RC coupled common emitter amplifier using a BC547 transistor. The aim is to determine its voltage gain, input impedance, frequency response, and bandwidth.

metadata.canonical: Experiment 1: RC Coupled Common Emitter Amplifier

text: BC547 Transistor. BC547 transistor. The specific NPN bipolar junction transistor used as the active amplifying component in the common emitter amplifier circuit.

Voltage Gain (Av). Voltage gain. A primary parameter to be determined in the experiment, representing the ratio of the output voltage to the input voltage (Av = Vout / Vin). The experiment measured a mid-frequency gain of approximately 28.4.
Input Impedance (Rin). input impedance = 2 kΩ. A parameter to be determined for the amplifier. The design target for the input impedance is specified as 2 kΩ.

Frequency Response. frequency response. A key characteristic to be studied, describing how the amplifier's gain varies with the frequency of the input signal. The response shows low gain at low/high frequencies and constant gain at mid-frequencies.

Bandwidth. Bandwidth. A parameter to be determined, representing the range of frequencies over which the amplifier's gain is within a specified range of its maximum value. The calculated bandwidth is approximately 400 kHz.
Sine Wave Input Signal. sine wave input signal. The type of signal used as the input for testing the amplifier. A specific amplitude of 500 mV peak-to-peak is used.

Regulated DC power supply. Regulated DC power supply. Apparatus required to provide a stable DC voltage to power the amplifier circuit. The specified supply voltage is 12 V, though the procedure mentions 15 V.
Function generator. Functiongenerator. Apparatus required to generate the sine wave input signal for the amplifier over a frequency range of 1 Hz to 10 MHz.
CRO / DSO. CRO / DSO. Cathode Ray Oscilloscope or Digital Storage Oscilloscope. This apparatus is required to observe and measure the input and output waveforms of the amplifier.
Multimeter. multimeter. Apparatus required for making various electrical measurements in the circuit, such as DC voltages for biasing.
Breadboard. Breadboard. Apparatus used for assembling the temporary prototype of the amplifier circuit.
RC Coupled Amplifier. RC coupled amplifier. A type of voltage amplifier that uses resistors and capacitors to couple the AC signal between stages while blocking DC, preventing biasing interference.
Coupling Capacitors. coupling capacitors. Capacitors used to pass the AC signal from the input source to the amplifier's input and from the amplifier's output to the load, while blocking DC voltage.

Common Emitter Amplifier. COMMON EMITTER AMPLIFIER. A specific transistor amplifier configuration where the emitter terminal is common to both the input and output circuits. It is characterized by high voltage gain and a 180-degree phase inversion.
Emitter Terminal. emitter terminal. One of the three terminals of the BC547 transistor. In this configuration, it is common to both input and output.
Base Terminal. Base. One of the three terminals of the BC547 transistor. The input signal is applied to this terminal.
Collector Terminal. Collector. One of the three terminals of the BC547 transistor. The amplified output signal is taken from this terminal.

Phase Inversion of 180 degrees. phase
inversion of 180 degrees. A characteristic of the common emitter amplifier where the output signal is 180 degrees out of phase with the input signal.

Voltage Divider Bias Network. voltage divider bias network. A biasing circuit, using resistors R1 (100 kΩ) and R2 (18 kΩ), designed to set a stable DC operating point (Q-point) for the transistor in the active region.

Transistor Operation in Active Region. active region. The desired operating mode for the transistor to function as an amplifier, where a small change in base voltage causes a large, proportional change in collector current.

Collector Resistor (Rc). Collector resistor. A resistor connected to the collector terminal, used to convert the amplified variations in collector current into a corresponding output voltage variation. Its value is 1.5 kΩ.

Amplifier Gain Dependence on Frequency. gain of an RC coupled amplifier depends on frequency. The principle that the voltage gain of an RC coupled amplifier is not constant across all frequencies. It is low at very low and very high frequencies and maximum in a mid-frequency range.
Low-Frequency Range. low frequencies. The range of frequencies where the amplifier gain is reduced due to the high reactance of coupling and bypass capacitors.

Mid-Frequency Range. mid
frequencies. The range of frequencies where the amplifier gain is nearly constant and at its maximum value. In the experiment, this is observed from approximately 500 Hz to 200 kHz.
High-Frequency Range. high frequencies. The range of frequencies where the amplifier gain decreases due to the effects of internal transistor capacitances and the Miller effect.

High Reactance of Capacitors. reactance of coupling and bypass capacitors is high. At low frequencies, the impedance (reactance) of coupling and bypass capacitors becomes significant, which causes a voltage drop and reduces the overall gain of the amplifier.

Internal Transistor Capacitances. internal transistor capacitances. Parasitic capacitances inherent within the transistor structure (e.g., between base-collector, base-emitter) that limit the amplifier's performance at high frequencies.

Miller Effect. Miller effect. An effect in inverting amplifiers where the capacitance between the input and output terminals appears as a much larger capacitance at the input, limiting the high-frequency response.

Procedure Step 1: Assemble Circuit. Assemble the RC coupled common emitter amplifier circuit on a breadboard. The first step in the experimental procedure, which involves building the RC coupled common emitter amplifier circuit on a breadboard.

Procedure Step 2: Apply DC Supply. Apply a DC supply voltage of 15 V to the circuit. The second step in the procedure, applying a DC supply voltage of 15 V to the circuit. This value conflicts with the 12 V listed in the components table.

Procedure Step 3: Connect Function Generator. Connect the function generator to the input through the coupling capacitor. The third step in the procedure, connecting the signal source to the amplifier's input via the input coupling capacitor.

Procedure Step 4: Apply Input Signal. Apply a sine wave input of 500 mV peak-to-peak. The fourth step in the procedure, applying a sine wave with an amplitude of 500 mV peak-to-peak to the amplifier's input.
Collector Resistor 1.5 kΩ. Collector resistor 1.5 kΩ. The specific value of the collector resistor (Rc) used in the circuit.
Emitter Resistor 1 kΩ. Emitter resistor 1 kΩ. The specific value of the emitter resistor (Re) used in the circuit for biasing stability.
Base Resistor R1 100 kΩ. Base resistor R1 100 kΩ. The specific value of the upper resistor (R1) in the voltage divider bias network.
Base Resistor R2 18 kΩ. Base resistor R2 18 kΩ. The specific value of the lower resistor (R2) in the voltage divider bias network.
Input Capacitor 1 µF. Input capacitor 1 µF. The specific value of the input coupling capacitor (Cin) used in the circuit.
Output Capacitor 1 µF. Output capacitor 1 µF. The specific value of the output coupling capacitor (Cout) used in the circuit.
Emitter Bypass Capacitor 22 µF. Emitter bypass capacitor 22 µF. The specific value of the emitter bypass capacitor (Ce) used to bypass the emitter resistor at AC frequencies to increase gain.
Supply Voltage 12 V. Supply voltage 12 V. The specified DC supply voltage (Vcc) for the circuit as listed in the components table. This value conflicts with the 15 V mentioned in the procedure.
Input Voltage 500 mV p-p. Input voltage 500 mV p-p. The peak-to-peak amplitude of the sine wave input signal used for testing the amplifier.
Frequency Range 1 Hz – 10 MHz. Frequency range 1 Hz – 10 MHz. The range of frequencies over which the input signal was varied to study the amplifier's frequency response.

Check BC547 Pin Configuration. Check BC547 pin configuration. A precaution to be taken before connecting the transistor to ensure the emitter, base, and collector leads are correctly identified and placed in the circuit.
Rated Supply Voltage. rated supply voltage. A precaution advising not to exceed the maximum specified supply voltage for the components, particularly the BC547 transistor, to avoid damage.

Input Signal Amplitude. input signal amplitude. A precaution to keep the input signal amplitude small to ensure the amplifier operates in its linear region and avoids output signal clipping or distortion.

Observe Correct Polarity of Electrolytic Capacitors. Observe correct polarity of electrolytic capacitors. A precaution to ensure that polarized capacitors, such as electrolytic types used for coupling and bypassing, are connected with the correct positive and negative polarity.

metadata.canonical: Observe Correct Polarity of Electrolytic Capacitors

text: Expected Behavior. expected behavior. The theoretical performance of the RC coupled common emitter amplifier, which the experimental results are compared against and found to match.

Block DC Components. blocking DC
components. A primary function of coupling capacitors in an AC amplifier, which is to prevent the DC bias voltage of one stage from affecting the DC bias of the next stage.

Convert Current Variations to Voltage Variations. converts these
current variations into voltage variations. The primary function of the collector resistor (Rc). As the transistor's collector current varies in response to the input signal, this current flows through Rc, creating a varying voltage drop (V=IR), which forms the output voltage signal.

metadata.canonical: Convert Current Variations to Voltage Variations
